title: Understanding clostridial diseases and vaccination
subtitle: The group of diseases prevented by the standard sheep vaccine, and why the timing matters.
excerpt: The annual clostridial vaccination is the single most cost-effective health intervention in sheep keeping. The eight diseases it covers are, in most cases, fatal within hours of first symptoms. Understanding which diseases the vaccine prevents and when to give it is more useful than recognising the symptoms, because by the time symptoms appear, treatment is rarely possible.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, Clostridial Disease factsheet (ahdb.org.uk). SAC Consulting Scotland, Sheep Health Notes. DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Sheep, 2020.

glossaryTerms.definition: A group of fatal bacterial diseases caused by Clostridium species. In sheep, the most significant are pulpy kidney (Cl. perfringens type D), lamb dysentery (Cl. perfringens type B), tetanus (Cl. tetani), braxy (Cl. septicum), and blackleg (Cl. chauvoei). All are preventable by the combined 8-in-1 vaccine.

An additional dose of vaccine given to maintain immunity in a previously vaccinated animal. For the clostridial vaccine in sheep, ewes need an annual booster 4-6 weeks before lambing to pass maternal immunity to the lamb via colostrum.

content.text: vaccine (sold in the UK under several brand names, commonly referred to as the 7-in-1 or 8-in-1) prevents a group of bacterial diseases that collectively represent the most common cause of sudden death in sheep without obvious prior illness. Unvaccinated lambs on good spring grass are at particular risk of pulpy kidney; unvaccinated adults are at risk of tetanus after any wound or birthing injury.

Pulpy kidney (enterotoxaemia type D) is the most frequently encountered. It kills fast-growing lambs abruptly, often the best lambs in the flock, when a sudden change to rich pasture causes Clostridium perfringens to proliferate rapidly in the gut. The lamb is found dead with no warning signs. Lamb dysentery (type B) causes bloody diarrhoea and rapid death in lambs under 3 weeks. Tetanus follows contaminated wounds; castration and tailing are the most common routes in young lambs.
The primary course and the booster timing
Unvaccinated adults need a primary course of two injections given 4-6 weeks apart. After the primary course, an annual

maintains immunity. For ewes, the booster timing is the critical decision: give it 4-6 weeks before the expected lambing date. This elevates the ewe's antibody level at exactly the point when her colostrum is being produced, maximising the passive immunity she passes to the lamb.
Lambs: when direct vaccination takes effect

Lambs that received adequate colostrum from a well-vaccinated ewe are protected for the first 8-12 weeks of life by maternal antibodies. After that, the protection wanes. In a flock where lambs are sold before 12 weeks, no further vaccination of lambs is necessary. Lambs kept beyond 12 weeks need their own primary course starting at 8-12 weeks to ensure continuous protection through the fast-growing autumn period when pulpy kidney risk is highest.

Any ewe that joins the flock from outside without vaccination records is unvaccinated from a management perspective. Give her a primary course immediately, regardless of what her paperwork says, and plan her booster 4-6 weeks ahead of her first lambing on your holding.
